message.content: Adversaries may exploit unquoted file paths to hijack execution flow by placing malicious executables in higher-level directories. This vulnerability arises when paths lack surrounding quotations, allowing Windows to mistakenly execute the adversary's payload instead of the intended program. Common targets include service paths and shortcut paths with spaces that are not enclosed in quotation marks. This technique can facilitate persistence if the executable is regularly called and can lead to privilege escalation if the intercepted executable is initiated by a higher-privileged process. Tools like Empire and PowerSploit contain modules to discover and exploit these vulnerabilities. Mitigation strategies include ensuring all file paths in system configurations and scripts are properly quoted, regularly auditing and correcting unquoted paths, and employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized executables from running.
